planting.note: Direct sow in fall in California and mild-winter gardens; seeds germinate through winter and bloom in early spring.

directions: Five-spot is a cheerful California native annual with trailing stems and white, bowl-shaped flowers, each petal tipped with a bold violet-purple spot that inspired the common name. It is native to moist valley floors and shaded woodland edges in California's coastal ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills. Sow seed in fall directly into moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to filtered sun, pressing seeds lightly into the surface without covering. It pairs beautifully with California poppies and baby blue eyes, and self-sows reliably in cool, moist conditions to return each spring.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery Native Plant Database

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, sandy or gravelly well-drained soil; seeds germinate with winter rains and bloom March through May.

directions: Parry's Phacelia is a southern California native annual bearing some of the most intensely colored flowers of any native plant — vivid, deep violet-purple, bell-shaped blooms clustered in coiling, fiddlehead-style cymes atop upright, glandular-hairy stems. It is native to dry, rocky, and sandy soils in coastal scrub and chaparral of Los Angeles, San Diego, and Baja California, blooming from March through May. Direct sow in fall on bare, dry, sandy, or gravelly well-drained soil in full sun; no soil amendment is needed and rich soil reduces flowering. Handle plants with gloves as the hairy stems can cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals, and expect native bees — including specialist phacelia bees — to visit heavily.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist shade; cold stratification over winter improves germination. Plants bloom in their second year.

directions: Appendaged Waterleaf is a native biennial or short-lived perennial of the waterleaf family, found in moist, shaded deciduous woodlands and rich floodplain forests across the eastern and central United States. In its first year it produces a rosette of large, pinnately lobed, soft-hairy leaves often decorated with pale whitish mottling; in the second year it sends up branched stems topped with rounded clusters of tubular, pale lavender to white flowers with conspicuously long-exserted stamens that give the blooms a feathery, soft appearance in spring. Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade, and allow the colony to self-sow for a self-sustaining planting. It is an underused native for shaded wild gardens and woodland restoration plantings in zones 3-8.

planting.note: In desert gardens, sow on the soil surface after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm; seeds germinate rapidly after warm rains.

directions: Bristly Nama is a tiny, prostrate native annual that forms dense, bristly mats of narrow, hairy leaves completely blanketed with small, vibrant purple to magenta-purple, funnel-shaped flowers from spring through fall following desert rains. It is native to sandy flats, dry washes, and open disturbed ground in Arizona, New Mexico, Texas, and adjacent Mexico, where it is one of the most cheerful and reliable wildflowers of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran deserts after monsoon events. Scatter seed on bare, dry, sandy, well-drained soil in full sun with no soil preparation needed — it thrives in lean conditions that would challenge most plants. It is an excellent low-growing native annual for desert garden edges, dry sandy slopes, and any hot, arid problem area where conventional plants fail.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate with spring warmth.

directions: Frying Pans is a diminutive California native annual closely related to the California poppy but considerably smaller, with finely dissected, blue-green foliage and tiny, bright yellow, 4-petaled flowers barely half an inch across that spread wide and flat — earning the whimsical common name from their resemblance to a miniature skillet. It is native to dry, rocky foothill grasslands and open chaparral in the Sacramento Valley and Sierra Nevada foothills, where it often grows in thin, gravelly soils with little competition. Direct sow in fall by scattering seed on dry, lean, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun; like its relatives, it resents transplanting and performs best with no soil amendment. It self-sows freely and makes an excellent low-growing native filler for California dry-garden borders, rock gardens, and native meadow mixes.
